retrieved_docs.title: Deep Plane Facelift -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Pre-operative photography: static and dynamic images in a frontal, three-quarter, and lateral views should adequately document ear position/shape, facial asymmetries, hairlines, and patterns of muscle action. The "chin-down" position is very useful to assess the effectiveness of any facelift on the cervico-mental angle and the neck in general.

retrieved_docs.title: Ptosis Correction -- Introduction -- Clinical examination
retrieved_docs.content: Clinical examination starts from the moment the patient walks into the doctor's clinic. It is essential to look for any facial asymmetry, frontalis overaction, chin up or head tilt posture.

retrieved_docs.title: VNG/ENG Testing -- Procedures -- Positioning Test
retrieved_docs.content: The positioning test, or Dix-Hallpike maneuver, is performed by rotating the patient's head 45° laterally from neutral and briskly transitioning the patient from a seated to supine position while maintaining head rotation. This movement should result in neck extension over the end of the examination table with the examiner supporting the patient's head. The patient's eye movements should be observed for at least 20 and as much as 60 seconds. Nystagmus is a positive response to the Dix-Hallpike maneuver. Historically, a positive response would warrant repeating the maneuver to document fatigue of the nystagmus; this is no longer warranted in all circumstances. However, the maneuver should be repeated with the head turned to the opposite side.

retrieved_docs.title: Strabismus -- Evaluation -- Compensator Head Posture (CHP)
retrieved_docs.content: CHP is a motor adaptation to strabismus to attain binocular single vision (BSV). Loss of CHP in concomitant strabismus may indicate the loss of BSV and warrants surgical intervention. In acquired paretic strabismus, CHP eliminates diplopia and helps to centralize the binocular visual field. Head tilt, face turn, and chin up/down are commonly seen as abnormal head postures. A head tilt to the left is seen in the right superior oblique palsy. A face turn to the left is seen in left lateral rectus palsy. Chin up or down is seen in the "A" or "V" pattern strabismus. [46]

retrieved_docs.title: Facial Chin Augmentation -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Although these methods provide valuable insight into achieving an ideal chin position concerning the lips, face, and neck, only a few account for the crucial relationship between the nose and chin. This relationship is vital to acknowledge, as they directly influence each other's relative appearance in space. As a general rule, a more projected nose tends to make the chin appear less projected, and vice versa. An essential and ideal harmonious balance exists between the projection of the chin and the nose. Notably, all the mentioned analysis methods rely on standardized photography techniques, with four of them utilizing the Frankfort horizontal line—a well-established anatomical reference extending from the superior border of the external auditory canal to the inferior orbital rim. [5] Furthermore, radiographic evaluation, including panoramic radiographs and/or lateral and anterior-posterior (AP) radiographs, may be beneficial for addressing more complex deformities such as vertical height excess, orthognathic abnormalities, and transverse asymmetry, commonly observed in conditions such as hemifacial/craniofacial microsomia, oculoauricular vertebral (OAV) syndrome, or isolated anatomical components.

retrieved_docs.title: The Spherical Equivalent -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Employed for the correction of sphero-cylindrical errors among patients with atypical/anomalous head posture (e.g., torticollis) or certain forms of compensatory head tilt (such as marked chin-up posture among individuals with globe elevation deficits). retrieved_docs.content: The MCA is commonly defined as the angle between a line passing through the nasal tip and pogonion and a tangential line through the submental point, with an ideal angle ranging from 110º to 120º. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthetic Considerations In Bariatric Surgery -- Issues of Concern -- Intraoperative
retrieved_docs.content: A common misconception is that the patient's adipose tissue is an adequate cushion and prevents pressure injuries. The body habitus can obscure positioning mistakes and leave the patient vulnerable to entrapment injury, nerve damage, and rhabdomyolysis. Positioning devices may include a footboard or stirrups in the steep Trendelenburg or modified Lloyd–Davies positions. The extremities should be secured and padded in a neutral position. Shifting, compression, or overstretching can cause injury (eg, sprained or fractured ankles, sciatic or brachial plexus injury). Positioning can be done before induction of anesthesia to allow the patient to aid in positioning for comfort and safety. Muscle paralysis during anesthesia often masks painful positions that patients are not flexible enough to accommodate awake. Repeated checks during the procedure should be done to ensure they remain free from entrapment and undue pressure or stretch. Pre-induction airway preparation includes the patient being ramped with positioning cushions or blankets to provide optimal airway visualization with or without video laryngoscopy. These devices are removed after intubation to prevent brachial plexus stretch injuries. Alternatively, the patient bed may be placed in an approximately 25- to 40-degree reverse Trendelenburg position to decrease the compression of the lung structures and improve ventilatory mechanics as much as possible during preoxygenation when the patient is spontaneously breathing. Caution should be used when relying on reverse Trendelenburg alone to prepare for intubation, as this position may not compensate for other difficulties in the patient's airway.

retrieved_docs.title: Cervical Plexus Block -- Technique or Treatment -- Patient Positioning
retrieved_docs.content: The patient can be placed into 1 of the following positions: supine or semi-sitting, both with head turned to the contralateral side, or lateral decubitus. The lateral decubitus position allows the ultrasound to be placed across from the patient for optimal ergonomics and viewing of the display screen. The patient's comfort and limitations must be taken into consideration during positioning. In patients who are obese, the posterior border of the SCM can be challenging to identify. The patient can lift their head off the bed to facilitate palpation of the posterior border of the SCM.

retrieved_docs.title: Pretrichial Brow Lift -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: When brow lifting and blepharoplasty are undertaken for functional reasons, documenting visual field deficits is crucial for interactions with third-party payers. Equally important is the establishment of standard preoperative photography. Essential images encompass frontal views in repose and with brow elevation, at a minimum. If blepharoplasty is part of the plan, additional profile views of the eyes in neutral, closed, and upward positions may prove beneficial. Beyond aiding in surgical planning and serving as medicolegal documentation when paired with postoperative images, these preoperative photographs should be prominently displayed in the operating room during the procedure for easy reference.

retrieved_docs.title: Autorefractors -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The patient should be explained to keep the arms on the table, chin on the chinrest, and head should rest against the forehead rest. Then the chin adjustment should be made to align the visual axis. The height can be moved up and down with a height adjustment knob. Each step should be explained to the patient while operating the autorefractor.

Recognizing this presentation is important for obstetric assessment and delivery planning, as it influences the choice of birth method and the management of potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2904
retrieved_docs.content: Malposition; malpresentation are specific manifestations of fetal position instability (عدم استقرار وضعية الجنين). Both terms describe abnormal fetal positioning that can compromise the progress of labor and increase the risk of delivery complications. Recognizing that malposition and malpresentation are subcategories of fetal position instability helps clinicians anticipate potential problems, monitor the pregnancy more closely, and plan appropriate obstetric interventions to ensure safer outcomes for both mother and infant.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal and fetal conditions that influence labor and delivery encompass a broad spectrum of obstetric complications, uterine functional abnormalities, cervical pathology, multiple‑gestation pregnancies, fetal size disproportion, abnormal fetal presentations, and drug contraindications in pregnancy. Abnormal fetal presentations—breech malpresentation, face or brow presentation, mentum (chin‑first) presentation, transverse or oblique lie, arm presentation (سوء المجيء الجنيني, تدلّي ذراع الجنين), shoulder presentation (وضعية المَجيء الكتفي), funic presentation, and other malpositions or unstable fetal positions—can impede vaginal descent and often require operative delivery or specialized obstetric management; they are linked to prolonged first‑stage or second‑stage labor, failure to induce labor, uterine hypotonia (رحم ناقص التوتر), secondary uterine inertia (عطالة رحمية ثانوية), and prolapse of the fetal arm. Anterior and posterior asynclitism represent specific uterine functional

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 614
retrieved_docs.content: Face OR brow presentation of fetus is organized under the broader category “Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable,” which in turn is classified as a type of malposition; malpresentation. This malposition; malpresentation category is a central classification within “Indications for care in pregnancy; labor; and delivery,” indicating that fetal malpositions are important considerations in obstetric management. Malposition; malpresentation also classifies other specific fetal position abnormalities, including “Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” and “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable.” The entity “Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” has the parent term “تدلّي ذراع الجنين,” the Arabic expression for a prolapsed fetal arm, while “Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus” is linked to its parent “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable.” Additionally, the relationship “has parent” connects the concept of malposition; malpresentation with the Arabic phrase “عدم استقرار وضعية الجنين,” which translates to “instability of fetal position,” reinforcing that these malpresentations represent unstable fetal orientations. Collectively, these entities and their hierarchical relationships delineate how various fetal presentations—face or brow, prolapsed arm, transverse or oblique—are categorized under the overarching malposition; malpresentation framework, highlighting their relevance for clinical decision‑making during pregnancy, labor, and delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2044
retrieved_docs.content: Face OR brow presentation of fetus is a specific fetal presentation that has as its parent the broader category Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable. This broader category is classified as a Malposition; malpresentation, indicating that the fetus is positioned abnormally in the birth canal. Understanding that both Face OR brow presentation of fetus and the unspecified Face or brow presentation fall under the umbrella of malposition or malpresentation informs clinicians that these presentations may complicate labor and delivery, prompting careful obstetric assessment and management.
